chunk_summary: This section defines nutrition challenges in chronic cardiorespiratory diseases, emphasizing India's malnutrition burden and the interplay of disease-driven catabolism, reduced intake, and baseline undernutrition in clinical management.
learning_objectives: Understand the physiological basis of nutrition challenges in chronic cardiorespiratory diseases.
Differentiate between baseline undernutrition and disease-driven malnutrition in Indian pediatric populations.
Identify key factors influencing nutrition targets and delivery routes in conditions like BPD, CHD, and CF.
key_facts: Chronic lung disease (CLD) in this context refers to BPD or CLD of prematurity, assessed at 36 weeks postmenstrual age.
India's NFHS-5 (2019–21) reports high rates of stunting (35.5%), wasting (19.3%), and underweight (32.1%) in children under five.
Disease-driven malnutrition in conditions like CHD, BPD, or CF requires active intervention, not dismissal as 'constitutional small size.'
Higher energy expenditure and protein catabolism in chronic cardiorespiratory diseases are driven by factors like work of breathing, inflammation, and sympathetic activation.
Reduced intake in these diseases is often due to fatigue, dyspnea, caregiver burden, and feeding skill limitations.
reference_quote: Nationally, NFHS-5 (2019–21) reports high rates of stunting, wasting, and underweight in under-fives, so clinicians must separate background undernutrition from disease-driven catabolism and delivery failure.
document_key_topics: Nutrition challenges in chronic cardiorespiratory diseases
Clinical management of BPD, CHD, and CF
